subject: maths
title: Introduction to Three Dimensional Geometry
book: NCERT Class 11 maths — Introduction to Three Dimensional Geometry

nodes.title: Set up the three coordinate axes and three coordinate planes in space
nodes.brief: To work in space, three mutually perpendicular lines (axes) meeting at a single point (origin) define three coordinate planes and a rectangular coordinate system.
nodes.quote: These three planes intersect along the lines X'OX, Y'OY and Z'OZ, called the *x*, *y* and *z*-axes, respectively.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Name the three mutually perpendicular axes meeting at origin O.
Take three planes that are mutually perpendicular to each other, intersecting at a single point O. They intersect in pairs along three lines X'OX, Y'OY and Z'OZ, called the x-, y- and z-axes. Together these lines form the rectangular coordinate system. The point O where all three axes meet is the origin.

PART 2 — Identify the three coordinate planes from the axes.
Taken in pairs, the three axes determine three coordinate planes: the XY-plane (containing the x- and y-axes), the YZ-plane (containing y and z), and the ZX-plane (containing z and x). Every point in space is located relative to these three planes.

PART 3 — Fix the sign convention along each axis.
By convention the XY-plane is taken as horizontal (the plane of the paper) and the z-axis is vertical. Distances measured upward along OZ are positive; downward along OZ' are negative. Similarly, forward along OX is positive and to the right along OY is positive.
keyMoves: Name the three mutually perpendicular lines X'OX, Y'OY, Z'OZ as the x, y and z-axes meeting at the origin O
Identify the three coordinate planes XY (XOY), YZ (YOZ) and ZX (ZOX) determined by the axes taken in pairs
Fix the sign convention along each axis (e.g. up along OZ positive, down along OZ' negative)
misconceptions: Thinking two axes (as in the plane) are enough to locate a point in space
Confusing an axis (a line) with a coordinate plane (determined by a pair of axes)

nodes.title: Identify the eight octants the coordinate planes divide space into
nodes.brief: Recognise that the three coordinate planes cut space into eight regions called octants, numbered I to VIII.
nodes.quote: The three coordinate planes divide the space into eight parts known as *octants*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State that the three coordinate planes divide space into eight octants.
Just as two axes in a plane divide it into four quadrants, three mutually perpendicular coordinate planes divide all of space into eight parts called octants. They are denoted I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII. The three planes together create 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 distinct regions.

PART 2 — Associate the first octant with all three coordinates positive.
The first octant, named XOYZ, is the region where all three coordinates are positive: x > 0, y > 0, z > 0. It is the natural starting octant, and every other octant is obtained by flipping one or more signs.

PART 3 — Recognise each octant as one combination of signs of x, y and z.
Table 11.1 of NCERT lists the sign pattern for every octant: Octant I is (+,+,+), Octant II is (−,+,+), Octant III is (−,−,+), Octant IV is (+,−,+), Octant V is (+,+,−), Octant VI is (−,+,−), Octant VII is (−,−,−) and Octant VIII is (+,−,−). Knowing a point's coordinate signs immediately tells you which octant it is in.
keyMoves: State that the three coordinate planes divide space into eight octants (denoted I to VIII)
Associate the first octant (XOYZ) with all three coordinates positive
Recognise each octant as one combination of the signs of x, y and z
misconceptions: Saying the coordinate planes divide space into four parts (confusing octants with the plane's quadrants)
Treating the octant numbering as arbitrary rather than tied to the signs of the coordinates

nodes.title: Associate a point in space with an ordered triplet (x, y, z)
nodes.brief: Read off the three coordinates of a point as its perpendicular distances from the three coordinate planes, giving a one-to-one correspondence between points and triplets (x, y, z).
nodes.quote: Thus, to each point P in the space there corresponds an ordered triplet (x, y, z) of real numbers.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Drop perpendiculars to obtain x, y, z as coordinates of P.
Given point P in space, drop perpendicular PM onto the XY-plane with foot M. Then from M drop perpendicular ML to the x-axis with foot L. Set OL = x, LM = y, MP = z. These are the x-, y- and z-coordinates of P, written P(x, y, z). Alternatively, draw planes through P parallel to each coordinate plane; where they meet the x-, y- and z-axes gives OA = x, OB = y, OC = z.

PART 2 — Interpret x, y, z as perpendicular distances from the coordinate planes.
The x-coordinate of P is its perpendicular distance from the YZ-plane (positive if P is on the OX side). The y-coordinate is its perpendicular distance from the ZX-plane. The z-coordinate is its perpendicular distance from the XY-plane. This geometric meaning is the definition, not the construction.

PART 3 — Use the one-to-one correspondence between points and triplets.
Every point P in space yields a unique ordered triplet (x, y, z), and conversely every triplet (x, y, z) locates exactly one point. So (1, 2, 3) and (3, 2, 1) are two different points. The order matters.
keyMoves: Drop perpendiculars to obtain x, y, z as the coordinates of P (write P(x, y, z))
Interpret x, y, z as the perpendicular distances of P from the YZ, ZX and XY planes respectively
Use the one-to-one correspondence: each triplet (x, y, z) locates exactly one point and vice versa
misconceptions: Pairing each coordinate with the wrong plane (e.g. taking x as the distance from the XY-plane instead of the YZ-plane)
Treating (x, y, z) as an unordered set, so that e.g. (1, 2, 3) and (3, 2, 1) name the same point

nodes.title: Write the coordinate form of points lying on an axis or a coordinate plane
nodes.brief: Use the special forms of coordinates for points that lie on a coordinate axis (one nonzero coordinate) or in a coordinate plane (one zero coordinate).
nodes.quote: The coordinates of any point on the *x*-axis will be as (x,0,0) and the coordinates of any point in the YZ-plane will be as (0, y, z).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write a point on the x-, y- or z-axis as (x,0,0), (0,y,0) or (0,0,z).
A point on the x-axis lies at the intersection of the XY-plane and the ZX-plane, so both y = 0 and z = 0. Its coordinates are (x, 0, 0). Similarly, a point on the y-axis has x = 0 and z = 0, giving (0, y, 0), and a point on the z-axis has x = 0 and y = 0, giving (0, 0, z). An axis point has TWO zero coordinates.

PART 2 — Write a point in a coordinate plane with the remaining coordinate zero.
A point in the XY-plane has z = 0 (it is at zero perpendicular distance from that plane), while x and y are free: form (x, y, 0). A point in the YZ-plane has x = 0: form (0, y, z). A point in the ZX-plane has y = 0: form (x, 0, z). A plane-point has exactly ONE zero coordinate.

PART 3 — Use these forms to decide which axis or plane a given point lies on.
Given a point, count its zero coordinates: two zeros means it is on an axis (identify which axis by the nonzero entry), one zero means it is in a coordinate plane, zero zeros means it is in an octant interior. For example (0, −4, 7) has x = 0 and lies in the YZ-plane.
keyMoves: Write a point on the x-, y-, z-axis as (x,0,0), (0,y,0), (0,0,z) respectively
Write a point in the XY-, YZ-, ZX-plane with the remaining coordinate zero (e.g. XY-plane: (x, y, 0))
Use these forms to decide which axis or plane a given point lies on
misconceptions: Setting only one coordinate to zero for an axis point, when a point on an axis needs two zero coordinates
Confusing a point in a plane (one zero coordinate) with a point on an axis (two zero coordinates)

nodes.title: Determine the octant of a point from the signs of its coordinates
nodes.brief: Use the sign of each coordinate, together with the octant sign table, to name the octant in which a given point lies.
nodes.quote: The sign of the coordinates of a point determine the octant in which the point lies.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read off the sign (+ or −) of each coordinate x, y, z.
For a given point, determine whether each coordinate is positive or negative. For example, (−3, 1, 2) has signs (−, +, +). A zero coordinate means the point lies on a coordinate plane boundary and does not belong strictly to any octant.

PART 2 — Match the sign pattern to Table 11.1 to name the octant.
NCERT Table 11.1 lists the sign combination for all eight octants: I = (+,+,+), II = (−,+,+), III = (−,−,+), IV = (+,−,+), V = (+,+,−), VI = (−,+,−), VII = (−,−,−), VIII = (+,−,−). Match your pattern to this table. The point (−3, 1, 2) has pattern (−, +, +) which is Octant II. The point (−3, 1, −2) has pattern (−, +, −) which is Octant VI.

PART 3 — Recognise that a zero coordinate puts the point on a coordinate plane boundary.
If any one of x, y, z equals zero, the point lies on a coordinate plane (the boundary between octants) and is not strictly inside any single octant. If two coordinates are zero, the point is on an axis. Only a point with all three coordinates nonzero belongs to exactly one octant.
keyMoves: Read off the sign (+ or -) of each of x, y, z for the given point
Match the sign pattern to Table 11.1 to name the octant (e.g. (-, +, +) is octant II)
Recognise that a zero coordinate puts the point on a coordinate plane, not inside a single octant
misconceptions: Reading the wrong octant by mixing up the order of the coordinate signs
Assuming a point with a zero coordinate still lies strictly inside one octant

nodes.title: Compute the distance between two points using the 3D distance formula
nodes.brief: Apply PQ = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2 + (z2-z1)^2) to find the distance between two points in space.
nodes.quote: This gives us the distance between two points $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2, z_2)$.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Subtract corresponding coordinates to get the three differences.
For P(x1, y1, z1) and Q(x2, y2, z2), the differences are (x2 − x1), (y2 − y1) and (z2 − z1). These are the three edge lengths of a rectangular box (parallelopiped) whose main diagonal is PQ. For P(1, −3, 4) and Q(−4, 1, 2): differences are −5, 4, −2.

PART 2 — Square each difference, add the three squares, and take the positive square root.
Apply Pythagoras twice: first on the base face to get the face diagonal d, then on the vertical to get the space diagonal PQ. The result is PQ = sqrt((x2−x1)^2 + (y2−y1)^2 + (z2−z1)^2). For the example: 25 + 16 + 4 = 45, so PQ = sqrt(45).

PART 3 — Simplify the surd to its simplest form.
Factor out perfect squares from under the root. sqrt(45) = sqrt(9 × 5) = 3 sqrt(5). Always state the final distance in simplified surd form. The order of the two points does not matter because each difference is squared: (x2 − x1)^2 = (x1 − x2)^2.
keyMoves: Subtract corresponding coordinates to get (x2-x1), (y2-y1), (z2-z1)
Square each difference, add the three squares, and take the positive square root
Simplify the surd (e.g. sqrt(45) = 3 sqrt(5))
misconceptions: Dropping the z-term and using only the two-dimensional distance formula
Forgetting to square the coordinate differences, or taking a negative square root

nodes.title: Find the distance of a point from the origin
nodes.brief: Specialise the distance formula to the origin: OQ = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2).
nodes.quote: which gives the distance between the origin O and any point Q
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise the origin as O(0, 0, 0) and substitute into the distance formula.
The distance-from-origin formula is the special case of the general distance formula where the first point is O(0, 0, 0). Setting x1 = y1 = z1 = 0 gives PQ → OQ = sqrt((x−0)^2 + (y−0)^2 + (z−0)^2). No subtraction is needed since the origin contributes zero to every difference.

PART 2 — Compute OQ = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2) by squaring and adding.
Simply square the three coordinates of Q and add them, then take the square root. For Q(1, 2, 2): OQ = sqrt(1 + 4 + 4) = sqrt(9) = 3. For Q(3, 4, 0): OQ = sqrt(9 + 16 + 0) = sqrt(25) = 5 (the familiar 3-4-5 triangle now in 3D with z = 0).

PART 3 — Take the positive square root to get the distance.
Distance is always non-negative. After squaring and adding, take the positive square root only. The distance from the origin is zero only for the origin itself, since x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 0 implies x = y = z = 0.
keyMoves: Recognise the origin as O(0, 0, 0) and set the first point to O in the distance formula
Compute OQ = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2) by squaring the point's coordinates and adding
Take the positive square root to get the distance
misconceptions: Still subtracting coordinates when one point is the origin (the origin contributes zeros)
Summing the coordinates instead of summing their squares

nodes.title: Test whether three points are collinear using distances
nodes.brief: Decide if three points lie on a line by checking whether the largest pairwise distance equals the sum of the other two.
nodes.quote: We know that points are said to be collinear if they lie on a line.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the three pairwise distances using the distance formula.
Given three points P, Q, R, compute all three pairwise distances PQ, QR and PR with the 3D distance formula. For P(−2, 3, 5), Q(1, 2, 3), R(7, 0, −1): PQ = sqrt(9+1+4) = sqrt(14); QR = sqrt(36+4+16) = sqrt(56) = 2 sqrt(14); PR = sqrt(81+9+36) = sqrt(126) = 3 sqrt(14).

PART 2 — Check whether the largest distance equals the sum of the other two.
Identify the largest of the three distances (here PR = 3 sqrt(14)). Test: does PQ + QR = PR? Here sqrt(14) + 2 sqrt(14) = 3 sqrt(14) = PR. Yes. If the equality holds, the middle point (Q) lies between the outer two on the line, confirming all three are collinear.

PART 3 — Conclude collinear if the sum condition holds, else they form a triangle.
If the largest distance equals the sum of the other two, the three points are collinear. If strict inequality holds (sum > largest), the triangle inequality tells you the three points form a genuine triangle and are not collinear. Note: if two distances are equal and the third is smaller, you have an isosceles triangle, not collinear points.
keyMoves: Compute the three pairwise distances PQ, QR, PR with the distance formula
Check whether the largest distance equals the sum of the other two
Conclude collinear if PQ + QR = PR (else they form a triangle)
misconceptions: Adding the wrong pair of distances (not putting the largest distance on its own side)
Concluding collinearity from equal distances alone rather than from the sum condition

nodes.title: Classify a triangle (right-angled, isosceles) from its side lengths
nodes.brief: Use the three side lengths from the distance formula to decide whether a triangle is right-angled (Pythagoras) or isosceles (two equal sides).
nodes.quote: Hence, the triangle ABC is not a right angled triangle.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the squared side lengths AB^2, BC^2, CA^2 from the distance formula.
For three given vertices, compute the square of each side length using the distance formula. Using squares avoids simplifying surds and is exactly what the Pythagorean relation needs. For A(3, 6, 9), B(10, 20, 30), C(25, −41, 5): AB^2 = 49 + 196 + 441 = 686; BC^2 = 225 + 3721 + 625 = 4571; CA^2 = 484 + 2209 + 16 = 2709.

PART 2 — Test for a right angle: largest squared side = sum of the other two?
Identify the largest squared side (BC^2 = 4571). Check: CA^2 + AB^2 = 2709 + 686 = 3395 ≠ 4571. No other pairing works either, so the triangle is not right-angled. If the check had succeeded (say AB^2 + BC^2 = CA^2), the right angle would be at B (the vertex not on the longest side).

PART 3 — Test for isosceles: are two of the squared side lengths equal?
If any two squared side lengths are equal, those two sides are equal in length and the triangle is isosceles. For A(0,7,−10), B(1,6,−6), C(4,9,−6): AB^2 = 1+1+16 = 18 and BC^2 = 9+9+0 = 18, so AB = BC and the triangle is isosceles with apex at B.
keyMoves: Compute the squared side lengths AB^2, BC^2, CA^2 from the distance formula
Test for a right angle: does the largest squared side equal the sum of the other two (converse of Pythagoras)?
Test for isosceles: are two of the side lengths equal?
misconceptions: Checking only one pairing for Pythagoras instead of testing the largest side against the other two
Confusing the isosceles test (two equal sides) with the right-angle test (Pythagoras relation)

nodes.title: Find the equation of a set of points (locus) from a distance condition
nodes.brief: Let P be (x, y, z), translate a given distance condition into an equation using the distance formula, and simplify to the equation of the set of points.
nodes.quote: Find the equation of the set of the points P such that its distances from the points A (3, 4, -5) and B (-2, 1, 4) are equal.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Let the variable point be P(x, y, z) and write each distance with the distance formula.
The key move is to introduce a general variable point P = (x, y, z) and express each relevant distance in terms of x, y, z using the formula. For points equidistant from A(3, 4, −5) and B(−2, 1, 4): PA = sqrt((x−3)^2 + (y−4)^2 + (z+5)^2) and PB = sqrt((x+2)^2 + (y−1)^2 + (z−4)^2).

PART 2 — Impose the given condition and square to remove square roots.
Set up the equation from the condition. For PA = PB, squaring both sides gives PA^2 = PB^2: (x−3)^2 + (y−4)^2 + (z+5)^2 = (x+2)^2 + (y−1)^2 + (z−4)^2. Squaring is valid because distances are non-negative.

PART 3 — Expand and collect terms to get the equation of the locus.
Expand both sides and cancel x^2, y^2, z^2 terms (which appear on both sides). Collect remaining terms: −10x − 6y + 18z + 29 = 0, which multiplied by −1 gives 10x + 6y − 18z − 29 = 0. This is the perpendicular bisector plane of AB in 3D space.
keyMoves: Let the variable point be P(x, y, z) and write each required distance with the distance formula
Impose the given condition (e.g. PA = PB, or PA^2 + PB^2 = 2k^2) and square to remove roots
Expand and collect terms to get the equation of the set of points
misconceptions: Setting the distances themselves equal without squaring, leaving awkward square roots
Algebra slips when expanding (x +/- a)^2 terms, e.g. mishandling the sign of the cross term

nodes.title: Relate a triangle's centroid to its vertices' coordinates
nodes.brief: Use that each centroid coordinate is the average of the corresponding coordinates of the three vertices to find a missing vertex or the centroid.
nodes.quote: The centroid of a triangle ABC is at the point (1, 1, 1).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write each centroid coordinate as the average of that coordinate over the three vertices.
The centroid G of a triangle with vertices A(x1, y1, z1), B(x2, y2, z2), C(x3, y3, z3) is G = ((x1+x2+x3)/3, (y1+y2+y3)/3, (z1+z2+z3)/3). This extends the 2D centroid formula directly to 3D: each coordinate of G is independently the average of the three corresponding vertex coordinates.

PART 2 — Set up one equation per coordinate to find an unknown vertex or the centroid.
If the centroid and two vertices are given and you need the third, set up three separate equations — one per coordinate — from the averaging relation. For G(1,1,1), A(3,−5,7), B(−1,7,−6): the three equations are (3−1+x)/3 = 1, (−5+7+y)/3 = 1, (7−6+z)/3 = 1.

PART 3 — Solve the three equations for the unknown vertex (or evaluate for the centroid).
Each equation is linear in one unknown: (2+x)/3 = 1 gives x = 1; (2+y)/3 = 1 gives y = 1; (1+z)/3 = 1 gives z = 2. So C = (1, 1, 2). Always work coordinate by coordinate — never mix x, y, z averages together.
keyMoves: Write each centroid coordinate as the average of that coordinate over the three vertices
Set up one equation per coordinate (e.g. (x1 + x2 + x3)/3 = Gx)
Solve the three equations for the unknown vertex (or evaluate them to get the centroid)
misconceptions: Averaging only two of the three vertices, or forgetting to divide by 3
Mixing coordinates across axes instead of averaging x with x, y with y, z with z

nodes.title: Locate points in space and solve distance problems in three dimensional geometry
nodes.brief: Combine the coordinate frame, octants and the distance formula to locate any point in space and to solve problems on collinearity, triangles, loci and centroids.
nodes.quote: In this Chapter, we shall study the basic concepts of geometry in three dimensional space.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Locate a point from its coordinates: octant from signs, special form on axis or plane.
Given P(x, y, z), determine its position qualitatively first: check the signs of x, y, z against Table 11.1 to name the octant. If any coordinate is zero, identify the axis or plane the point lies on. Special forms: (x, 0, 0) is on the x-axis; (0, y, z) is in the YZ-plane. Example: (−4, 2, −5) has signs (−, +, −) so it lies in Octant VI.

PART 2 — Apply the distance formula for separations, distance from origin, collinearity and triangle type.
The distance formula PQ = sqrt((x2−x1)^2 + (y2−y1)^2 + (z2−z1)^2) is the computational engine for this chapter. From it: distance from origin OQ = sqrt(x^2+y^2+z^2); collinearity test PQ + QR = PR; triangle classification (largest squared side = sum of others for right angle; two equal sides for isosceles). Example: P(1,−3,4) to Q(−4,1,2) gives PQ = sqrt(45) = 3 sqrt(5).

PART 3 — Translate a distance condition into a locus equation, and use the centroid averaging relation.
For a locus: let P = (x, y, z), express the given distances, impose the condition, square to remove roots, expand and collect. For the centroid: G = ((x1+x2+x3)/3, (y1+y2+y3)/3, (z1+z2+z3)/3); solve coordinate by coordinate for any unknown. Both techniques reduce a geometric description to an algebraic equation in x, y and z.
keyMoves: Locate a point from its coordinates: octant from the signs, special form if it lies on an axis or plane
Apply the distance formula to get separations, distance from the origin, collinearity and triangle type
Translate a distance condition into the equation of a set of points, and use the centroid averaging relation
misconceptions: Reverting to two-dimensional reasoning (two coordinates, plane distance) when the problem is in space
Treating each result (octant, distance, collinearity, locus, centroid) as a separate trick rather than as one coordinate-plus-distance framework
